sections.label: Section 364
sections.original: κακῶς πέπρακται πανταχῇ · τίς ἀντερεῖ ;
sections.translation: On all sides sorrow pens me in . Who shall gainsay this ?

sections.label: Section 365
sections.original: ἀλλ ’ οὔτι ταύτῃ ταῦτα , μὴ δοκεῖτέ πω .
sections.translation: But all is not yet lost ! think not so . Still are there troubles in store for the new bride , and for her bridegroom no light toil . Dost think I would ever have fawned on yonder man , unless to gain some end or form some scheme ?

sections.label: Section 370
sections.original: οὐδ ’ ἂν προσεῖπον οὐδ ’ ἂν ἡψάμην χεροῖν .
sections.translation: Nay , I would not so much as have spoken to him or touched him with my hand . But he has in folly so far stepped in that , though he might have checked my plot by banishing me from the land , he hath allowed me to abide this day , in which I will lay low in death three of my enemies —

sections.label: Section 375
sections.original: θήσω , πατέρα τε καὶ κόρην πόσιν τ ’ ἐμόν .
sections.translation: a father and his daughter and my husband too . Now , though I have many ways to compass their death , I am not sure , friends , which I am to try first . Shall I set fire to the bridal mansion , or plunge the whetted sword through their hearts ,

sections.label: Section 380
sections.original: σιγῇ δόμους ἐσβᾶσ ’, ἵν ’ ἔστρωται λέχος .
sections.translation: softly stealing into the chamber where their couch is spread ? One thing stands in my way . If I am caught making my way into the chamber , intent on my design , I shall be put to death and cause my foes to mock . ’ Twere best to take the shortest way — the way we women are

sections.label: Section 385
sections.original: σοφαὶ μάλιστα , φαρμάκοις αὐτοὺς ἑλεῖν .
sections.translation: most skilled in — by poison to destroy them . Well , well , suppose them dead ; what city will receive me ? What friendly host will give me a shelter in his land , a home secure , and save my soul alive ? None . So I will wait yet a little while

sections.label: Section 390
sections.original: ἢν μέν τις ἡμῖν πύργος ἀσφαλὴς φανῇ ,
sections.translation: in case some tower of defence rise up for me ; then will I proceed to this bloody deed in crafty silence ; but if some unexpected mischance drive me forth , I will with mine own hand seize the sword , e ’ en though I die for it , and slay them , and go forth on my bold path of daring .

sections.label: Section 395
sections.original: οὐ γὰρ μὰ τὴν δέσποιναν ἣν ἐγὼ σέβω
sections.translation: By that dread queen whom I revere before all others and have chosen to share my task , by Hecate who dwells within my inmost chamber , not one of them shall wound my heart and rue it not . Bitter and sad will I make their marriage for them ;

sections.label: Section 400
sections.original: πικρὸν δὲ κῆδος καὶ φυγὰς ἐμὰς χθονός .
sections.translation: bitter shall be the wooing of it , bitter my exile from the land . Up , then , Medea , spare not the secrets of thy art in plotting and devising ; on to the danger . Now comes a struggle needing courage . Dost see what thou art suffering ? ’ Tis not for thee to be a laughing-stock

sections.label: Section 405
sections.original: τοῖς Σισυφείοις τοῖς τ ’ Ἰάσονος γάμοις ,
sections.translation: to the race of Sisyphus [*] by reason of this wedding of Jason , sprung , as thou art , from a noble sire , and of the Sun-god ’ s race . Thou hast cunning ; and , more than this , we women , though by nature little apt for virtuous deeds , are most expert to fashion any mischief .

sections.label: Section 1.410
sections.original: ἄνω ποταμῶν ἱερῶν χωροῦσι παγαί ,
sections.translation: Back to their source the holy rivers turn their tide . Order and the universe are being reversed . ’ Tis men whose counsels are treacherous , whose oath by heaven is no longer sure .

sections.label: Section 1.415
sections.original: τὰν δ ’ ἐμὰν εὔκλειαν ἔχειν βιοτὰν στρέψουσι φᾶμαι ·
sections.translation: Rumour shall bring a change o ’ er my life , bringing it into good repute . Honour ’ s dawn is breaking for woman ’ s sex ;

sections.label: Section 1.420
sections.original: οὐκέτι δυσκέλαδος φάμα γυναῖκας ἕξει .
sections.translation: no more shall the foul tongue of slander fix upon us .

sections.label: Section 1.421
sections.original: μοῦσαι δὲ παλαιγενέων λήξουσ ’ ἀοιδῶν
sections.translation: The songs of the poets of old shall cease to make our faithlessness their theme . Phoebus , lord of minstrelsy , hath not implanted in our mind

sections.label: Section 1.425
sections.original: ὤπασε θέσπιν ἀοιδὰν
sections.translation: the gift of heavenly song , else had I sung an answering strain to the race of males , for time ’ s long chapter affords

sections.label: Section 1.430
sections.original: πολλὰ μὲν ἁμετέραν ἀνδρῶν τε μοῖραν εἰπεῖν .
sections.translation: many a theme on their sex as well as ours .

sections.label: Section 2.431
sections.original: σὺ δ ’ ἐκ μὲν οἴκων πατρίων ἔπλευσας
sections.translation: With mind distraught didst thou thy father ’ s house desert on thy voyage betwixt ocean ’ s twin rocks , and on a foreign

sections.label: Section 2.435
sections.original: ναίεις χθονί , τᾶς ἀνάνδρου
sections.translation: strand thou dwellest , thy bed left husbandless , poor lady and thou an exile from the land , dishonoured , persecuted .

sections.label: Section 2.439
sections.original: βέβακε δ ’ ὅρκων χάρις , οὐδ ’ ἔτ ’ αἰδὼς
sections.translation: Gone is the grace that oaths once had . Through all the breadth

sections.label: Section 2.440
sections.original: Ἑλλάδι τᾷ μεγάλᾳ μένει , αἰθερία δ ’ ἀνέπτα .
sections.translation: of Hellas honour is found no more ; to heaven hath it sped away . For thee no father ’ s house is open , woe is thee ! to be a haven from the troublous storm , while o ’ er thy home is set another queen , the bride that is

sections.label: Section 2.445
sections.original: δόμοισιν ἐπέστα .
sections.translation: preferred to thee .

sections.label: Section 446
sections.original: οὐ νῦν κατεῖδον πρῶτον ἀλλὰ πολλάκις
sections.translation: It is not now I first remark , but oft ere this , how unruly a pest is a harsh temper . For instance , thou , hadst thou but patiently endured the will of thy superiors , mightest have remained here in this land and house ,

sections.label: Section 450
sections.original: λόγων ματαίων οὕνεκ ’ ἐκπεσῇ χθονός .
sections.translation: but now for thy idle words wilt thou be banished . Thy words are naught to roe . Cease not to call Jason basest of men ; but for those words thou hast spoken against our rulers , count it all gain that exile is thy only punishment .

sections.label: Section 455
sections.original: κἀγὼ μὲν αἰεὶ βασιλέων θυμουμένων
sections.translation: I ever tried to check the outbursts of the angry monarch , and would have had thee stay , but thou wouldst not forego thy silly rage , always reviling our rulers , and so thou wilt be banished . Yet even after all this I weary not of my goodwill ,

sections.label: Section 460
sections.original: ἥκω , τὸ σὸν δὲ προσκοπούμενος , γύναι ,
sections.translation: but am come with thus much forethought , lady , that thou mayst not be destitute nor want for aught , when , with thy sons , thou art cast out . Many an evil doth exile bring in its train with it ; for even though thou hatest me , never will I harbour hard thoughts of thee .

sections.label: Section 465
sections.original: ὦ παγκάκιστε , τοῦτο γάρ σ ’ εἰπεῖν ἔχω ,
sections.translation: Thou craven villain ( for that is the only name my tongue can find for thee , a foul reproach on thy unmanliness )! comest thou to me , thou , most hated foe of gods , of me , and of all mankind ? Tis no proof of courage or hardihood

sections.label: Section 470
sections.original: φίλους κακῶς δράσαντ ’ ἐναντίον βλέπειν ,
sections.translation: to confront thy friends after injuring them , but that worst of all human diseases — loss of shame . Yet hast thou done well to come ; for I shall ease ray soul by reviling thee , and thou wilt be vexed at my recital .

sections.label: Section 475
sections.original: ἐκ τῶν δὲ πρώτων πρῶτον ἄρξομαι λέγειν .
sections.translation: I will begin at the very beginning . I saved thy life , as every Hellene knows who sailed with thee aboard the good ship Argo , when thou wert sent to tame and yoke fire-breathing bulls , and to sow the deadly tilth .

sections.label: Section 480
sections.original: δράκοντά θ ’, ὃς πάγχρυσον ἀμπέχων δέρας
sections.translation: Yea , and I slew the dragon which guarded the golden fleece , keeping sleepless watch o ’ er it with many a wreathed coil , and I raised for thee a beacon of deliver arice . Father and home of my free will I left and came with thee to Iolcos , ’ neath Pelion ’ s hills ,

sections.label: Section 485
sections.original: σὺν σοί , πρόθυμος μᾶλλον ἢ σοφωτέρα ·
sections.translation: for my love was stronger than my prudence . Next I caused the death of Pelias by a doom most grievous , even by his own children ’ s hand , beguiling them of all their fear . All this have I done for thee , thou traitor ! and thou hast cast me over , taking to thyself another wife ,

sections.label: Section 490
sections.original: παίδων γεγώτων · εἰ γὰρ ἦσθ ’ ἄπαις ἔτι ,
sections.translation: though children have been bom to us . Hadst thou been childless still , I could have pardoned thy desire for this new union .

sections.label: Section 492
sections.original: ὅρκων δὲ φρούδη πίστις , οὐδ ’ ἔχω μαθεῖν
sections.translation: Gone is now the trust I put in oaths . I cannot even understand whether thou thinkest that the gods of old no longer rule , or that fresh decrees are now in vogue amongst mankind ,

sections.label: Section 495
sections.original: ἐπεὶ σύνοισθά γ ’ εἰς ἔμ ’ οὐκ εὔορκος ὤν .
sections.translation: for thy conscience must tell thee thou hast not kept faith with me . Ah ! poor right hand , which thou didst often grasp . These knees thou didst embrace ! All in vain , I suffered a traitor to touch me ! How short of my hopes I am fallen ! But come , I will deal with thee as though thou wert my friend .

sections.label: Section 500
sections.original: — δοκοῦσα μὲν τί πρός γε σοῦ πράξειν καλῶς ;
sections.translation: Yet what kindness can I expect from one so base as thee ? but yet I will do it , for my questioning will show thee yet more base . Whither can I turn me now ? to my father ’ s house , to my own country , which I for thee deserted to come hither ? to the hapless daughters of Pelias ? A glad

sections.label: Section 505
sections.original: δέξαιντό μ ’ οἴκοις ὧν πατέρα κατέκτανον .
sections.translation: welcome , I trow , would they give me in their home , whose father ’ s death I compassed ! My case stands even thus : I am become the bitter foe to those of mine own home , and those whom I need ne ’ er have wronged I have made mine enemies to pleasure thee . Wherefore to reward me for this thou hast made me doubly blest in the eyes of many a wife in Hellas ;

sections.label: Section 510
sections.original: ἔθηκας ἀντὶ τῶνδε · θαυμαστὸν δέ σε
sections.translation: and in thee I own a peerless , trusty lord . O woe is me , if indeed I am to be cast forth an exile from the land , without one friend ; one lone woman with her babes forlorn ! Yea , a fine reproach to thee in thy bridal hour ,

sections.label: Section 515
sections.original: πτωχοὺς ἀλᾶσθαι παῖδας ἥ τ ’ ἔσῳσά σε .
sections.translation: that thy children and the wife who saved thy life are beggars and vagabonds ! O Zeus ! why hast thou granted unto man clear signs to know the sham in gold , white on man ’ s brow no brand is stamped whereby to gauge the villain ’ s heart ?

sections.label: Section 520
sections.original: δεινή τις ὀργὴ καὶ δυσίατος πέλει ,
sections.translation: There is a something terrible and past all cure , when quarrels arise ’ twixt those who are near and dear .

sections.label: Section 522
sections.original: δεῖ μ ’, ὡς ἔοικε , μὴ κακὸν φῦναι λέγειν ,
sections.translation: Needs must I now , it seems , turn orator , and , like a good helmsman on a ship with close-reefed sails , weather

sections.label: Section 525
sections.original: τὴν σὴν στόμαργον , ὦ γύναι , γλωσσαλγίαν .
sections.translation: that wearisome tongue of thine . Now , I believe , since thou wilt exaggerate thy favours , that to Cypris alone of gods or men I owe the safety of my voyage . Thou hast a subtle wit enough ; yet were it a hateful thing for me

sections.label: Section 530
sections.original: λόγος διελθεῖν , ὡς Ἔρως σ ’ ἠνάγκασε
sections.translation: to say that the Love-god constrained thee by his resistless shaft to save my life . However , I will not reckon this too nicely ; ’ twas kindly done , however thou didst serve me . Yet for my safety

sections.label: Section 535
sections.original: εἴληφας ἢ δέδωκας , ὡς ἐγὼ φράσω .
sections.translation: hast thou received more than ever thou gavest , as I will show . First , thou dwellest in Hellas , instead of thy barbarian land , and hast learnt what justice means find how to live by law , not by the dictates of brute force ; and all the Hellenes recognize thy cleverness ,

sections.label: Section 540
sections.original: καὶ δόξαν ἔσχες · εἰ δὲ γῆς ἐπ ’ ἐσχάτοις
sections.translation: and thou hast gained a name ; whereas , if thou hadst dwelt upon the confines of the earth , no tongue had mentioned thee . Give me no gold within my halls ; nor skill to sing a fairer strain than ever Orpheus sang , unless therewith my fame be spread abroad !

sections.label: Section 545
sections.original: τοσαῦτα μέν σοι τῶν ἐμῶν πόνων πέρι
sections.translation: So much I say to thee about my own toils , for ’ twas thou didst challenge me to this retort . As for the taunts thou urgest against my marriage with the princess , I will prove to thee , first , that I am prudent herein , next chastened in my love , and last a powerful friend

sections.label: Section 550
sections.original: καὶ παισὶ τοῖς ἐμοῖσιν — ἀλλ ’ ἔχ ’ ἥσυχος .
sections.translation: to thee and to thy sons ; only hold thy peace . Since I have here withdrawn from Iolcos with many a hopeless trouble at my back , what happier device could I , an exile , frame than marriage with the daughter of the king ?

sections.label: Section 555
sections.original: οὐχ , ᾗ σὺ κνίζῃ , σὸν μὲν ἐχθαίρων λέχος ,
sections.translation: ’ Tis not because I loathe thee for my wife — the thought that rankles in thy heart ; ’ tis not because I am smitten with desire fot a new bride , nor yet that I am eager to vie with others in begetting many children , for those we have are quite enough , and I do not complain . Nay , ’ tis that we — and this is most important —

sections.label: Section 560
sections.original: καὶ μὴ σπανιζοίμεσθα , γιγνώσκων ὅτι
sections.translation: may dwell in comfort , instead of suffering want ( for well I know that every whilom friend avoids the poor ), and that I might rear my sons as doth befit my house ; further , that I might be the father of brothers for the children thou hast born , and raise these to the same high rank , uniting the family in one ,—

sections.label: Section 565
sections.original: εὐδαιμονοῖμεν . σοί τε γὰρ παίδων τί δεῖ ;
sections.translation: to my lasting bliss . Thou , indeed , hast no need of more children , but me it profits to help my present family by that which is to be . Have I miscarried here ? Not even thou wouldest say so unless a rival ’ s charms rankled in thy bosom . No , but you women have such strange ideas ,

sections.label: Section 570
sections.original: εὐνῆς γυναῖκες πάντ ’ ἔχειν νομίζετε ,
sections.translation: that you think all is well so long as your married life runs smooth ; but if some mischance occur to ruffle your love , all that was good and lovely erst you reckon as your foes . Yea , men should have begotten children from some other source , no female race existing ;

sections.label: Section 575
sections.original: χοὕτως ἂν οὐκ ἦν οὐδὲν ἀνθρώποις κακόν .
sections.translation: thus would no evil ever have fallen on mankind .

sections.label: Section 576
sections.original: Ἰᾶσον , εὖ μὲν τούσδ ’ ἐκόσμησας λόγους ·
sections.translation: This speech , O Jason , hast thou with specious art arranged ; but yet I think — albeit in saying so I betray indiscretion — that thou hast sinned in casting over thy wife .

sections.label: Section 579
sections.original: ἦ πολλὰ πολλοῖς εἰμι διάφορος βροτῶν .
sections.translation: No doubt I differ from the mass of men on many points ;

sections.label: Section 580
sections.original: ἐμοὶ γὰρ ὅστις ἄδικος ὢν σοφὸς λέγειν
sections.translation: for , to my mind , whoso hath skill to fence with words in an unjust cause , incurs the heaviest penalty ; for such an one , confident that he can cast a decent veil of words o ’ er his injustice , dares to practise it ; and yet he is not so very clever after all . So do not thou put forth thy specious pleas

sections.label: Section 585
sections.original: λέγειν τε δεινός . ἓν γὰρ ἐκτενεῖ σ ’ ἔπος ·
sections.translation: and clever words to me now , for one word of mine will lay thee low . Hadst thou not had a villain ’ s heart , thou shouldst have gained my consent , then made this match , instead of hiding it from those who loved thee .

sections.label: Section 588
sections.original: καλῶς γ ’ ἄν , οἶμαι , τῷδ ’ ὑπηρέτεις λόγῳ ,
sections.translation: Thou wouldest have lent me ready aid , no doubt , in this proposal , if I had told thee of my marriage , seeing that not even now

sections.label: Section 590
sections.original: τολμᾷς μεθεῖναι καρδίας μέγαν χόλον .
sections.translation: canst thou restrain thy soul ’ s hot fury .

sections.label: Section 591
sections.original: οὐ τοῦτό σ ’ εἶχεν , ἀλλὰ βάρβαρον λέχος
sections.translation: This was not what restrained thee ; but thine eye was turned towards old age , and a foreign wife began to appear discreditable to thee .

sections.label: Section 593
sections.original: εὖ νῦν τόδ ’ ἴσθι , μὴ γυναικὸς οὕνεκα
sections.translation: Be well assured of this : ’ twas not for the woman ’ s sake I wedded the king ’ s daughter , my present wife ;

sections.label: Section 595
sections.original: ἀλλ ’, ὥσπερ εἶπον καὶ πάρος , σῷσαι θέλων
sections.translation: but , as I have already told thee , I wished to insure thy safety and to be the father of royal sons bound by blood to my own children — a bulwark to our house .

sections.label: Section 598
sections.original: μή μοι γένοιτο λυπρὸς εὐδαίμων βίος
sections.translation: May that prosperity , whose end is woe , ne ’ er be mine , nor such wealth as would ever sting my heart !
